{{Short description|Japanese deities of good fortune}} [[File:Shichifukujin by Minzan-Ethno BHM 1995.266.49-P6141072-gradient.jpg|thumb|230px|Netsuke depicting the Seven gods of Fortune, on display at [[:en:Bern Historical Museum|Bern Historical Museum]]]] {{Jmyth infobox}} In Japanese mythology, the '''Seven Lucky gods''' or '''Seven gods of Fortune''' ({{lang|ja|七福神}}, {{lang|ja-latn|shichifukujin}} in [[Japanese language|Japanese]]) are believed to grant good [[luck]] and are often represented in [[netsuke]] and in artworks. One of the seven (Jurōjin) is said to be based on a historical figure.
They all began as remote and impersonal gods, but gradually became much closer canonical figures for certain professions and Japanese [[The arts|arts]]. During the course of their history, the mutual influence between gods has created confusion about which of them was the patron of certain professions. The worship of this group of gods is also due to the importance of the number seven in Japan, supposedly a signifier of good luck.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The seven lucky gods of Japan|last=Chiba|first=Reiko|year=1995}}</ref>
==Origin and history== [[File:Shichi fukujin.jpg|thumb|400px|right|From left to right: Hotei, Jurōjin, Fukurokuju, Bishamonten, Benzaiten, Daikokuten, Ebisu]] It is known that these deities mostly have their origins as ancient gods of fortune from religions popular in Japan: from [[Mahayana Buddhism]] (Benzaiten, Bishamonten, Daikokuten) which came to Japan from China but originated in India, and from Chinese [[Taoism]] (Fukurokuju, Hotei, Jurojin); except for one (Ebisu) who has a native Japanese ancestry.
These gods have been recognized as such for over a thousand years. In the beginning, these gods were worshiped by merchants as the first two (Ebisu and Daikokuten) were gods of [[business]] and trade.
Subsequently, the other classes of Japanese society looked for other gods that could correspond with their professions: Benzaiten as the patron of the arts, Fukurokuju as the patron of the sciences, and so on.
In ancient times, these gods were worshipped separately, but this rarely happens today – only when it is required for the god to act on behalf of the applicant.
The Seven Gods of Fortune started being mentioned as a collective in the year 1420 in Fushimi, in order to imitate the processions of the ''daimyōs'', the [[:en:Feudalism|feudal lords]] of pre-modern Japan.
It is said that the Buddhist priest [[Tenkai]] selected these gods after speaking with the ''[[shōgun]]'' he served, [[Iemitsu Tokugawa]], at the order of seeking whoever possessed the perfect virtues: longevity, fortune, popularity, sincerity, kindness, dignity, and magnanimity.
Shortly after a famous artist of the time, Kano Yasunobu, was ordained to portray these gods for the first time ever.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|year=2009}}</ref>
==Description of the Fukujin deities== [[File:Seven-Lucky-Gods-of-Japan-Hokusai-七福神.png|thumb|230px|Seven Lucky Gods by [[Hokusai]] et al.]] === Ebisu === {{Main|Ebisu (mythology)}} From the period of the gods [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]], Ebisu (恵比寿) is the only one whose origins are purely Japanese. He is the god of prosperity and wealth in business, and of plenitude and abundance in crops, cereals and food in general. He is the patron of [[:en:Fisherman|fishermen]] and therefore is represented with fishermen's costumes such as a typical hat, a fishing rod in his right hand and a fish that can be either a [[:en:Carp|Carp]], a [[hake]], a [[:en:Cod|Cod]]fish or a sea bass, or any large fish, in general, that symbolize abundance in meals (such as a feast or banquet). It is now common to see his figure in restaurants where fish is served in great quantities or in household kitchens.<ref name=":0" />
=== Daikokuten === {{Main|Daikokuten}} Daikokuten (大黒天) is the god of commerce and prosperity, and he is sometimes considered the patron of cooks, farmers and bankers, and a protector of [[crop]]s. He is also considered a [[:en:Demon|Demon]] hunter − legend says that the god Daikokuten hung a sacred talisman on the branch of a tree in his garden and, by using this as a trap, he was able to catch a demon. This god is characterized by his smile, by his short legs and by the hat on his head. He is usually depicted with a bag full of valuable objects.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Daikokuten's popular imagery originated as a [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|syncretic conflation]] of the [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[List of death deities#Hindu mythology|death deity]]{{Broken anchor|date=2024-06-01|bot=User:Cewbot/log/20201008/configuration|target_link=List of death deities#Hindu mythology|reason= The anchor (Hindu mythology) [[Special:Diff/871442714|has been deleted]].}} [[Mahākāla]] with the [[Kami|Shinto deity]] [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{cite book|first=Jeremy|last=Roberts|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|publisher=Infobase Publishing|year=2009|page=28|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC|isbn=9781438128023}}</ref> The Japanese name ''Daikoku'' is a direct translation of the Sanskrit name ''Mahākāla'' which means "Great Blackness". Per the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]] of 1690 (reprinted and expanded in 1796), Daikoku can also manifest as a female known as ''Daikokunyo'' (大黒女, lit. "She of Great Blackness") or ''Daikokutennyo'' (大黒天女, lit. "She of Great Blackness of the Heavens").<ref name="Butsuzōzui059">{{cite web|title=Butsuzōzui (Illustrated Compendium of Buddhist Images)|url=http://www.lib.ehime-u.ac.jp/SUZUKA/316/index.html|location=Ehime University Library|page=(059.jpg)|language=ja|format=digital photos|date=1796}}</ref>
=== Bishamonten === {{Main|Vaiśravana}} Bishamonten's (毘沙門天) origins can be traced back to [[Hinduism]], but he has been adopted into Japanese culture. He comes from the Hindu god [[Kubera]] and is also known by the name "[[Vaiśravaṇa|Vaisravana]]".
He is the god of fortune in war and battles, also associated with authority and dignity. He is the protector of those who follow the rules and behave appropriately. As the patron of fighters, he is represented dressed in [[:en:Armour|Armour]] and a helmet, and is often seen carrying a [[pagoda]]. He also acts as the protector of holy sites and important places and wields a spear to fight against the evil spirits. He is usually depicted in illustrations with a hoop of fire.
=== Benzaiten === {{Main|Benzaiten}} Benzaiten's (弁才天 or 弁財天) origin is found in [[Hinduism in Japan|Hinduism]], as she comes from the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]. She is the only female Fukujin in the modern grouping, and may be named in various ways: Benzaiten (弁才天), Benten (弁天), Bentensama (弁天様), or Benzaitennyo (弁才天女). When she was [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|adapted]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], she was given the attributes of financial fortune, talent, beauty and music among others. Often, her figure appears with a ''[[Torii]]''. She is represented as a smart, beautiful woman with all the aforementioned attributes. She carries a ''[[:en:Biwa|Biwa]]'', a Japanese traditional lute-like instrument, and is normally accompanied by a white snake. She is the patron of artists, writers, dancers, and [[geisha]], among others.<ref name=":0" />
=== Jurōjin === {{Main|Jurōjin}} Considered the incarnation of the southern [[pole star]] (南極星 "nankyokusei"), Juroujin (寿老人) is the god of the elderly and of longevity in Japanese Buddhist mythology. It is said that the legendary Juroujin is based on a real person who lived in ancient times. He was approximately 1.82 meters tall with a very long head. Besides his distinctive skull, he is represented with a long white beard, riding a deer, and is often also accompanied by a 1500-year-old [[:en:Crane (bird)|crane]] and a tortoise, as symbols of his affinity with long lives. In addition, he is usually represented under a peach tree, as the fruit of this tree is considered, by Chinese Taoism, as able to prolong life. In his hand he holds a cane and a book or a scroll. The wisdom of the world remains written in its pages. Jurojin enjoys rice and wine and is a very cheerful figure.<ref name=":0" />
=== Hotei === {{Main|Budai}} Hotei (布袋) is the god of fortune, guardian of children, patron of [[:en:Diviners|Diviners]] and barmen, and also the god of popularity. He is depicted as a fat, smiling, bald man with a curly moustache. He always appears half-naked, as his clothes are not wide enough to cover his enormous belly. He blessed the Chinese, and they nicknamed him "Cho-Tei-Shi" or "Ho-Tei-Shi", which means ‘bag of old clothes’.
Hotei was a Zen [[priest]], but his appearance and some of his actions were against their moral code: his appearance made him look like quite a mischievous person and he had no fixed place to sleep.
He carries a bag on his shoulders which is loaded with fortunes for those who believe in his virtues. Hotei's traits and virtues are contentment, magnanimity, and happiness.
Hotei's original Chinese name was Kaishi, and according to legend, he died in March 916.
The Japanese began to believe in Hotei during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo era]]. The reason why the Japanese have such great respect for this god comes from a legend that says that, before Zen Buddhism arrived in Japan, an alternative Buddhist thought was extended by a priest of dubious aesthetic, who actually was a manifestation of [[Maitreya|Miroku]]. Miroku was the patron of those who could not be saved by the beliefs of [[Gautama Buddha|Buddha]], and Hotei was later perceived and accepted by the Japanese as a second Miroku.<ref name=":0" />
=== Fukurokuju (sometimes omitted) === {{Main|Fukurokuju}} The god Fukurokuju (福禄寿) also has his origins in [[:en:China|China]]. It is believed that he used to be a [[hermit]] during the Chinese [[Song dynasty]], distinguished for being a reincarnation of the Taoist god Hsuan-wu. He is the god of wisdom, luck, longevity, wealth and happiness. This god receives certain credits, such as being one of the Chinese philosophers who could live without eating (breatharian). Moreover, he is the only god who was said to have the ability to resurrect the dead. Fukurokuju is characterized by the size of his head, being almost as large as the size of his whole body, and is represented wearing traditional Chinese costumes. He normally carries a cane in one hand and in the other a [[scroll]] with writings about the world. He is usually accompanied by a turtle, a crow or a deer, animals that are frequently used in Japan to symbolize a long life. It is also said that he likes to play [[:en:Chess|Chess]], and hence is the patron of chess players.<ref name=":0" /> The characteristics of Fukurokuju and Jurōjin overlap as they both trace back to the Chinese Taoist deity [[Old Man of the South Pole|Nánjílǎorén (南极老人)]], which is why Fukurokuju's position is sometimes granted instead to the goddess [[#Kichijōten (吉祥天)|Kichijōten]], as in the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]] of 1783.<ref name="Butsuzōzui077" />
=== Kichijōten (sometimes omitted)===
{{Main|Kisshōten}} Kichijōten (吉祥天), a Fukujin goddess is also known as Kisshōten or Kisshoutennyo (吉祥天女), and is adapted via [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] from the [[Hinduism in Japan|Hindu]] goddess [[Lakshmi]]. Kisshōten has the traits of beauty, happiness, and fertility. In the 1783 edition of the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]], Kichijōten replaces [[#Fukurokuju (福禄寿)|Fukurokuju]] as one of the Fukujin.<ref name="Butsuzōzui077">{{cite web|title=Butsuzōzui (Illustrated Compendium of Buddhist Images)|url=http://www.lib.ehime-u.ac.jp/SUZUKA/316/index.html|location=Ehime University Library|page=(077.jpg)|language=ja|format=digital photos|date=1796}}</ref> Kichijōten's iconography is distinguished from the other Fukujin goddesses by the [[Cintamani|Nyoihōju gem (如意宝珠)]] in her hand. When Kichijōten replaces Fukurokuju, and Daikoku is regarded in feminine form,<ref name="Butsuzōzui059" /> all three of the Hindu [[Tridevi]] goddesses are then represented among the seven Fukujin.
== Takarabune == [[File:Seven Lucky Gods (Shichi fukujin) in Treasure Ship by Utagawa Toyokuni I c1806.png|thumb|230px|Ukiyo-e of the lucky gods on a ''Takarabune'' by [[Utagawa Toyokuni]]]] {{Main|Takarabune}}
During the first three days of the [[New Year]] the Seven Lucky Gods are said to pilot through the heavens the ''[[Takarabune]]'' or Treasure Ship.<ref name="Chiba">{{Cite book |title=The Seven Lucky Gods of Japan |last=Reiko |first=Chiba |date=1966 |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co |pages=9–10 |oclc=40117755}}</ref> A picture of the ship forms an essential part of traditional [[Japanese New Year]] celebrations.<ref name="V&A">{{Cite web |url=https://collections.vam.ac.uk/item/O74843/the-treasure-ship-woodblock-print-utagawa-hiroshige |title=The Treasure Ship |publisher= [[Victoria and Albert Museum]] |access-date=2018-07-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180104014057/https://collections.vam.ac.uk/item/O74843/the-treasure-ship-woodblock-print-utagawa-hiroshige|url-status=dead|archive-date=4 January 2018}}</ref>
==Location of shrines== *Toka Ebisu Shrine, Fukuoka *Nanyo – [[Kanjizai-ji]], Shikoku * [[Nishinomiya Shrine]], Hyōgo * Imamiya Ebisu Shrine, Osaka
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:七福神-Shichifukujin.JPG|The Seven Lucky Gods at [[Watatsumi]] Shrine in [[Tarumi-ku, Kobe]] Image:Kuniyoshi Utagawa, The seven goods of good fortune.jpg|The Seven Lucky Gods in a woodblock print by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] Image:Yoshitoshi The Seven Lucky Gods.jpg|The Seven Lucky Gods, in an 1882 woodblock print by Tsukioka [[Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]] File:Seven Gods of Good Luck.jpg|Brooklyn Museum - Shibata Zeshin (Japanese, 1807−1891). Seven Gods of Good Luck, c. 1885 File:Takarabune by Hiroshige.png|Coloured woodblock print of the ''Takarabune'' by [[Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] </gallery>
== See also == {{Portal|Japan|Mythology}} * [[:en:Fu, Lu, and Shou|Fu, Lu, and Shou]] (福祿壽), three deities of stars from [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese folk religion]], representing auspiciousness, good life and longevity. * [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]], a similar group of [[Xian (Taoism)|Taoist immortals]] * [[He-He Er Xian]] (和合二仙), Immortals of Harmony and Union, associated with happy marriages
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==External links== *{{commonscat-inline|Seven Lucky Gods}} *[http://www.artelino.com/articles/japanese_gods_and_goddesses.asp Information on Japanese deities]

]] ]]

{{Short description|Japanese deities of good fortune}} [[File:Shichifukujin by Minzan-Ethno BHM 1995.266.49-P6141072-gradient.jpg|thumb|230px|Netsuke depicting the Seven gods of Fortune, on display at [[:en:Bern Historical Museum|Bern Historical Museum]]]] {{Jmyth infobox}} In Japanese mythology, the '''Seven Lucky gods''' or '''Seven gods of Fortune''' ({{lang|ja|七福神}}, {{lang|ja-latn|shichifukujin}} in [[Japanese language|Japanese]]) are believed to grant good [[luck]] and are often represented in [[netsuke]] and in artworks. One of the seven (Jurōjin) is said to be based on a historical figure.
They all began as remote and impersonal gods, but gradually became much closer canonical figures for certain professions and Japanese [[The arts|arts]]. During the course of their history, the mutual influence between gods has created confusion about which of them was the patron of certain professions. The worship of this group of gods is also due to the importance of the number seven in Japan, supposedly a signifier of good luck.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The seven lucky gods of Japan|last=Chiba|first=Reiko|year=1995}}</ref>
==Origin and history== [[File:Shichi fukujin.jpg|thumb|400px|right|From left to right: Hotei, Jurōjin, Fukurokuju, Bishamonten, Benzaiten, Daikokuten, Ebisu]] It is known that these deities mostly have their origins as ancient gods of fortune from religions popular in Japan: from [[Mahayana Buddhism]] (Benzaiten, Bishamonten, Daikokuten) which came to Japan from China but originated in India, and from Chinese [[Taoism]] (Fukurokuju, Hotei, Jurojin); except for one (Ebisu) who has a native Japanese ancestry.
These gods have been recognized as such for over a thousand years. In the beginning, these gods were worshiped by merchants as the first two (Ebisu and Daikokuten) were gods of [[business]] and trade.
Subsequently, the other classes of Japanese society looked for other gods that could correspond with their professions: Benzaiten as the patron of the arts, Fukurokuju as the patron of the sciences, and so on.
In ancient times, these gods were worshipped separately, but this rarely happens today – only when it is required for the god to act on behalf of the applicant.
The Seven Gods of Fortune started being mentioned as a collective in the year 1420 in Fushimi, in order to imitate the processions of the ''daimyōs'', the [[:en:Feudalism|feudal lords]] of pre-modern Japan.
It is said that the Buddhist priest [[Tenkai]] selected these gods after speaking with the ''[[shōgun]]'' he served, [[Iemitsu Tokugawa]], at the order of seeking whoever possessed the perfect virtues: longevity, fortune, popularity, sincerity, kindness, dignity, and magnanimity.
Shortly after a famous artist of the time, Kano Yasunobu, was ordained to portray these gods for the first time ever.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|year=2009}}</ref>
==Description of the Fukujin deities== [[File:Seven-Lucky-Gods-of-Japan-Hokusai-七福神.png|thumb|230px|Seven Lucky Gods by [[Hokusai]] et al.]] === Ebisu === {{Main|Ebisu (mythology)}} From the period of the gods [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]], Ebisu (恵比寿) is the only one whose origins are purely Japanese. He is the god of prosperity and wealth in business, and of plenitude and abundance in crops, cereals and food in general. He is the patron of [[:en:Fisherman|fishermen]] and therefore is represented with fishermen's costumes such as a typical hat, a fishing rod in his right hand and a fish that can be either a [[:en:Carp|Carp]], a [[hake]], a [[:en:Cod|Cod]]fish or a sea bass, or any large fish, in general, that symbolize abundance in meals (such as a feast or banquet). It is now common to see his figure in restaurants where fish is served in great quantities or in household kitchens.<ref name=":0" />
=== Daikokuten === {{Main|Daikokuten}} Daikokuten (大黒天) is the god of commerce and prosperity, and he is sometimes considered the patron of cooks, farmers and bankers, and a protector of [[:en:Crop|Crop]]s. He is also considered a [[:en:Demon|Demon]] hunter − legend says that the god Daikokuten hung a sacred talisman on the branch of a tree in his garden and, by using this as a trap, he was able to catch a demon. This god is characterized by his smile, by his short legs and by the hat on his head. He is usually depicted with a bag full of valuable objects.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Daikokuten's popular imagery originated as a [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|syncretic conflation]] of the [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[List of death deities#Hindu mythology|death deity]]{{Broken anchor|date=2024-06-01|bot=User:Cewbot/log/20201008/configuration|target_link=List of death deities#Hindu mythology|reason= The anchor (Hindu mythology) [[Special:Diff/871442714|has been deleted]].}} [[Mahākāla]] with the [[Kami|Shinto deity]] [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{cite book|first=Jeremy|last=Roberts|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|publisher=Infobase Publishing|year=2009|page=28|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC|isbn=9781438128023}}</ref> The Japanese name ''Daikoku'' is a direct translation of the Sanskrit name ''Mahākāla'' which means "Great Blackness". Per the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]] of 1690 (reprinted and expanded in 1796), Daikoku can also manifest as a female known as ''Daikokunyo'' (大黒女, lit. "She of Great Blackness") or ''Daikokutennyo'' (大黒天女, lit. "She of Great Blackness of the Heavens").<ref name="Butsuzōzui059">{{cite web|title=Butsuzōzui (Illustrated Compendium of Buddhist Images)|url=http://www.lib.ehime-u.ac.jp/SUZUKA/316/index.html|location=Ehime University Library|page=(059.jpg)|language=ja|format=digital photos|date=1796}}</ref>
=== Bishamonten === {{Main|Vaiśravana}} Bishamonten's (毘沙門天) origins can be traced back to [[Hinduism]], but he has been adopted into Japanese culture. He comes from the Hindu god [[Kubera]] and is also known by the name "[[Vaiśravaṇa|Vaisravana]]".
He is the god of fortune in war and battles, also associated with authority and dignity. He is the protector of those who follow the rules and behave appropriately. As the patron of fighters, he is represented dressed in [[:en:Armour|Armour]] and a helmet, and is often seen carrying a [[pagoda]]. He also acts as the protector of holy sites and important places and wields a spear to fight against the evil spirits. He is usually depicted in illustrations with a hoop of fire.
=== Benzaiten === {{Main|Benzaiten}} Benzaiten's (弁才天 or 弁財天) origin is found in [[Hinduism in Japan|Hinduism]], as she comes from the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]. She is the only female Fukujin in the modern grouping, and may be named in various ways: Benzaiten (弁才天), Benten (弁天), Bentensama (弁天様), or Benzaitennyo (弁才天女). When she was [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|adapted]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], she was given the attributes of financial fortune, talent, beauty and music among others. Often, her figure appears with a ''[[Torii]]''. She is represented as a smart, beautiful woman with all the aforementioned attributes. She carries a ''[[:en:Biwa|Biwa]]'', a Japanese traditional lute-like instrument, and is normally accompanied by a white snake. She is the patron of artists, writers, dancers, and [[geisha]], among others.<ref name=":0" />
=== Jurōjin === {{Main|Jurōjin}} Considered the incarnation of the southern [[pole star]] (南極星 "nankyokusei"), Juroujin (寿老人) is the god of the elderly and of longevity in Japanese Buddhist mythology. It is said that the legendary Juroujin is based on a real person who lived in ancient times. He was approximately 1.82 meters tall with a very long head. Besides his distinctive skull, he is represented with a long white beard, riding a deer, and is often also accompanied by a 1500-year-old [[:en:Crane (bird)|crane]] and a tortoise, as symbols of his affinity with long lives. In addition, he is usually represented under a peach tree, as the fruit of this tree is considered, by Chinese Taoism, as able to prolong life. In his hand he holds a cane and a book or a scroll. The wisdom of the world remains written in its pages. Jurojin enjoys rice and wine and is a very cheerful figure.<ref name=":0" />
=== Hotei === {{Main|Budai}} Hotei (布袋) is the god of fortune, guardian of children, patron of [[:en:Diviners|Diviners]] and barmen, and also the god of popularity. He is depicted as a fat, smiling, bald man with a curly moustache. He always appears half-naked, as his clothes are not wide enough to cover his enormous belly. He blessed the Chinese, and they nicknamed him "Cho-Tei-Shi" or "Ho-Tei-Shi", which means ‘bag of old clothes’.
Hotei was a Zen [[priest]], but his appearance and some of his actions were against their moral code: his appearance made him look like quite a mischievous person and he had no fixed place to sleep.
He carries a bag on his shoulders which is loaded with fortunes for those who believe in his virtues. Hotei's traits and virtues are contentment, magnanimity, and happiness.
Hotei's original Chinese name was Kaishi, and according to legend, he died in March 916.
The Japanese began to believe in Hotei during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo era]]. The reason why the Japanese have such great respect for this god comes from a legend that says that, before Zen Buddhism arrived in Japan, an alternative Buddhist thought was extended by a priest of dubious aesthetic, who actually was a manifestation of [[Maitreya|Miroku]]. Miroku was the patron of those who could not be saved by the beliefs of [[Gautama Buddha|Buddha]], and Hotei was later perceived and accepted by the Japanese as a second Miroku.<ref name=":0" />
=== Fukurokuju (sometimes omitted) === {{Main|Fukurokuju}} The god Fukurokuju (福禄寿) also has his origins in [[:en:China|China]]. It is believed that he used to be a [[hermit]] during the Chinese [[Song dynasty]], distinguished for being a reincarnation of the Taoist god Hsuan-wu. He is the god of wisdom, luck, longevity, wealth and happiness. This god receives certain credits, such as being one of the Chinese philosophers who could live without eating (breatharian). Moreover, he is the only god who was said to have the ability to resurrect the dead. Fukurokuju is characterized by the size of his head, being almost as large as the size of his whole body, and is represented wearing traditional Chinese costumes. He normally carries a cane in one hand and in the other a [[scroll]] with writings about the world. He is usually accompanied by a turtle, a crow or a deer, animals that are frequently used in Japan to symbolize a long life. It is also said that he likes to play [[:en:Chess|Chess]], and hence is the patron of chess players.<ref name=":0" /> The characteristics of Fukurokuju and Jurōjin overlap as they both trace back to the Chinese Taoist deity [[Old Man of the South Pole|Nánjílǎorén (南极老人)]], which is why Fukurokuju's position is sometimes granted instead to the goddess [[#Kichijōten (吉祥天)|Kichijōten]], as in the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]] of 1783.<ref name="Butsuzōzui077" />
=== Kichijōten (sometimes omitted)===
{{Main|Kisshōten}} Kichijōten (吉祥天), a Fukujin goddess is also known as Kisshōten or Kisshoutennyo (吉祥天女), and is adapted via [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] from the [[Hinduism in Japan|Hindu]] goddess [[Lakshmi]]. Kisshōten has the traits of beauty, happiness, and fertility. In the 1783 edition of the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]], Kichijōten replaces [[#Fukurokuju (福禄寿)|Fukurokuju]] as one of the Fukujin.<ref name="Butsuzōzui077">{{cite web|title=Butsuzōzui (Illustrated Compendium of Buddhist Images)|url=http://www.lib.ehime-u.ac.jp/SUZUKA/316/index.html|location=Ehime University Library|page=(077.jpg)|language=ja|format=digital photos|date=1796}}</ref> Kichijōten's iconography is distinguished from the other Fukujin goddesses by the [[Cintamani|Nyoihōju gem (如意宝珠)]] in her hand. When Kichijōten replaces Fukurokuju, and Daikoku is regarded in feminine form,<ref name="Butsuzōzui059" /> all three of the Hindu [[Tridevi]] goddesses are then represented among the seven Fukujin.
== Takarabune == [[File:Seven Lucky Gods (Shichi fukujin) in Treasure Ship by Utagawa Toyokuni I c1806.png|thumb|230px|Ukiyo-e of the lucky gods on a ''Takarabune'' by [[Utagawa Toyokuni]]]] {{Main|Takarabune}}
During the first three days of the [[New Year]] the Seven Lucky Gods are said to pilot through the heavens the ''[[Takarabune]]'' or Treasure Ship.<ref name="Chiba">{{Cite book |title=The Seven Lucky Gods of Japan |last=Reiko |first=Chiba |date=1966 |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co |pages=9–10 |oclc=40117755}}</ref> A picture of the ship forms an essential part of traditional [[Japanese New Year]] celebrations.<ref name="V&A">{{Cite web |url=https://collections.vam.ac.uk/item/O74843/the-treasure-ship-woodblock-print-utagawa-hiroshige |title=The Treasure Ship |publisher= [[Victoria and Albert Museum]] |access-date=2018-07-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180104014057/https://collections.vam.ac.uk/item/O74843/the-treasure-ship-woodblock-print-utagawa-hiroshige|url-status=dead|archive-date=4 January 2018}}</ref>
==Location of shrines== *Toka Ebisu Shrine, Fukuoka *Nanyo – [[Kanjizai-ji]], Shikoku * [[Nishinomiya Shrine]], Hyōgo * Imamiya Ebisu Shrine, Osaka
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:七福神-Shichifukujin.JPG|The Seven Lucky Gods at [[Watatsumi]] Shrine in [[Tarumi-ku, Kobe]] Image:Kuniyoshi Utagawa, The seven goods of good fortune.jpg|The Seven Lucky Gods in a woodblock print by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] Image:Yoshitoshi The Seven Lucky Gods.jpg|The Seven Lucky Gods, in an 1882 woodblock print by Tsukioka [[Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]] File:Seven Gods of Good Luck.jpg|Brooklyn Museum - Shibata Zeshin (Japanese, 1807−1891). Seven Gods of Good Luck, c. 1885 File:Takarabune by Hiroshige.png|Coloured woodblock print of the ''Takarabune'' by [[Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] </gallery>
== See also == {{Portal|Japan|Mythology}} * [[:en:Fu, Lu, and Shou|Fu, Lu, and Shou]] (福祿壽), three deities of stars from [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese folk religion]], representing auspiciousness, good life and longevity. * [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]], a similar group of [[Xian (Taoism)|Taoist immortals]] * [[He-He Er Xian]] (和合二仙), Immortals of Harmony and Union, associated with happy marriages
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==External links== *{{commonscat-inline|Seven Lucky Gods}} *[http://www.artelino.com/articles/japanese_gods_and_goddesses.asp Information on Japanese deities]

[[sn:Seven Lucky Gods]] [[en:Seven Lucky Gods]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:آلهة الحظ السبعة]] [[az:Yeddi Şanslı Tanrı]] [[ca:Set Déus de la Fortuna]] [[cs:Sedm bohů štěstí]] [[de:Sieben Glücksgötter]] [[es:Siete Dioses de la Fortuna]] [[fa:هفت خدای بخت و اقبال]] [[fi:Shichifukujin]] [[fr:Sept divinités du bonheur]] [[hr:Shichi Fukujin]] [[hu:Hét szerencseisten]] [[hy:Երջանկության յոթ աստվածներ]] [[it:Sette divinità della fortuna]] [[ja:七福神]] [[ko:칠복신]] [[my:ကံကြမ္မာနတ်ဘုရား ခုနစ်ပါး]] [[nl:Shichi Fukujin]] [[pl:Shichi-fukujin]] [[pt:Sete Deuses da Sorte]] [[ro:Cei șapte zei ai norocului]] [[ru:Семь богов счастья]] [[sh:Sedam božanstava sreće]] [[simple:Seven Lucky Gods]] [[th:เจ็ดเทพเจ้าโชคลาภ]] [[tr:Yedi Şanslı Tanrı]] [[uk:Сім богів щастя]] [[vi:Thất Phúc Thần]] [[zh:七福神]] [[zh-min-nan:Chhit-hok-sîn]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese deities of good fortune}} [[File:Shichifukujin by Minzan-Ethno BHM 1995.266.49-P6141072-gradient.jpg|thumb|230px|Netsuke depicting the Seven gods of Fortune, on display at [[:en:Bern Historical Museum|Bern Historical Museum]]]] {{Jmyth infobox}} In Japanese mythology, the '''Seven Lucky gods''' or '''Seven gods of Fortune''' ({{lang|ja|七福神}}, {{lang|ja-latn|shichifukujin}} in [[Japanese language|Japanese]]) are believed to grant good [[luck]] and are often represented in [[netsuke]] and in artworks. One of the seven (Jurōjin) is said to be based on a historical figure.
They all began as remote and impersonal gods, but gradually became much closer canonical figures for certain professions and Japanese [[The arts|arts]]. During the course of their history, the mutual influence between gods has created confusion about which of them was the patron of certain professions. The worship of this group of gods is also due to the importance of the number seven in Japan, supposedly a signifier of good luck.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The seven lucky gods of Japan|last=Chiba|first=Reiko|year=1995}}</ref>
==Origin and history== [[File:Shichi fukujin.jpg|thumb|400px|right|From left to right: Hotei, Jurōjin, Fukurokuju, Bishamonten, Benzaiten, Daikokuten, Ebisu]] It is known that these deities mostly have their origins as ancient gods of fortune from religions popular in Japan: from [[Mahayana Buddhism]] (Benzaiten, Bishamonten, Daikokuten) which came to Japan from China but originated in India, and from Chinese [[Taoism]] (Fukurokuju, Hotei, Jurojin); except for one (Ebisu) who has a native Japanese ancestry.
These gods have been recognized as such for over a thousand years. In the beginning, these gods were worshiped by merchants as the first two (Ebisu and Daikokuten) were gods of [[business]] and trade.
Subsequently, the other classes of Japanese society looked for other gods that could correspond with their professions: Benzaiten as the patron of the arts, Fukurokuju as the patron of the sciences, and so on.
In ancient times, these gods were worshipped separately, but this rarely happens today – only when it is required for the god to act on behalf of the applicant.
The Seven Gods of Fortune started being mentioned as a collective in the year 1420 in Fushimi, in order to imitate the processions of the ''daimyōs'', the [[:en:Feudalism|feudal lords]] of pre-modern Japan.
It is said that the Buddhist priest [[Tenkai]] selected these gods after speaking with the ''[[shōgun]]'' he served, [[Iemitsu Tokugawa]], at the order of seeking whoever possessed the perfect virtues: longevity, fortune, popularity, sincerity, kindness, dignity, and magnanimity.
Shortly after a famous artist of the time, Kano Yasunobu, was ordained to portray these gods for the first time ever.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|year=2009}}</ref>
==Description of the Fukujin deities== [[File:Seven-Lucky-Gods-of-Japan-Hokusai-七福神.png|thumb|230px|Seven Lucky Gods by [[Hokusai]] et al.]] === Ebisu === {{Main|Ebisu (mythology)}} From the period of the gods [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]], Ebisu (恵比寿) is the only one whose origins are purely Japanese. He is the god of prosperity and wealth in business, and of plenitude and abundance in crops, cereals and food in general. He is the patron of [[:en:Fisherman|fishermen]] and therefore is represented with fishermen's costumes such as a typical hat, a fishing rod in his right hand and a fish that can be either a [[:en:Carp|Carp]], a [[hake]], a [[:en:Cod|Cod]]fish or a sea bass, or any large fish, in general, that symbolize abundance in meals (such as a feast or banquet). It is now common to see his figure in restaurants where fish is served in great quantities or in household kitchens.<ref name=":0" />
=== Daikokuten === {{Main|Daikokuten}} Daikokuten (大黒天) is the god of commerce and prosperity, and he is sometimes considered the patron of cooks, farmers and bankers, and a protector of [[:en:Crop|Crop]]s. He is also considered a [[:en:Demon|Demon]] hunter − legend says that the god Daikokuten hung a sacred talisman on the branch of a tree in his garden and, by using this as a trap, he was able to catch a demon. This god is characterized by his smile, by his short legs and by the hat on his head. He is usually depicted with a bag full of valuable objects.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Daikokuten's popular imagery originated as a [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|syncretic conflation]] of the [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[List of death deities#Hindu mythology|death deity]]{{Broken anchor|date=2024-06-01|bot=User:Cewbot/log/20201008/configuration|target_link=List of death deities#Hindu mythology|reason= The anchor (Hindu mythology) [[Special:Diff/871442714|has been deleted]].}} [[Mahākāla]] with the [[Kami|Shinto deity]] [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{cite book|first=Jeremy|last=Roberts|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|publisher=Infobase Publishing|year=2009|page=28|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC|isbn=9781438128023}}</ref> The Japanese name ''Daikoku'' is a direct translation of the Sanskrit name ''Mahākāla'' which means "Great Blackness". Per the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]] of 1690 (reprinted and expanded in 1796), Daikoku can also manifest as a female known as ''Daikokunyo'' (大黒女, lit. "She of Great Blackness") or ''Daikokutennyo'' (大黒天女, lit. "She of Great Blackness of the Heavens").<ref name="Butsuzōzui059">{{cite web|title=Butsuzōzui (Illustrated Compendium of Buddhist Images)|url=http://www.lib.ehime-u.ac.jp/SUZUKA/316/index.html|location=Ehime University Library|page=(059.jpg)|language=ja|format=digital photos|date=1796}}</ref>
=== Bishamonten === {{Main|Vaiśravana}} Bishamonten's (毘沙門天) origins can be traced back to [[Hinduism]], but he has been adopted into Japanese culture. He comes from the Hindu god [[Kubera]] and is also known by the name "[[Vaiśravaṇa|Vaisravana]]".
He is the god of fortune in war and battles, also associated with authority and dignity. He is the protector of those who follow the rules and behave appropriately. As the patron of fighters, he is represented dressed in [[:en:Armour|Armour]] and a helmet, and is often seen carrying a [[pagoda]]. He also acts as the protector of holy sites and important places and wields a spear to fight against the evil spirits. He is usually depicted in illustrations with a hoop of fire.
=== Benzaiten === {{Main|Benzaiten}} Benzaiten's (弁才天 or 弁財天) origin is found in [[Hinduism in Japan|Hinduism]], as she comes from the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]. She is the only female Fukujin in the modern grouping, and may be named in various ways: Benzaiten (弁才天), Benten (弁天), Bentensama (弁天様), or Benzaitennyo (弁才天女). When she was [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|adapted]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], she was given the attributes of financial fortune, talent, beauty and music among others. Often, her figure appears with a ''[[Torii]]''. She is represented as a smart, beautiful woman with all the aforementioned attributes. She carries a ''[[:en:Biwa|Biwa]]'', a Japanese traditional lute-like instrument, and is normally accompanied by a white snake. She is the patron of artists, writers, dancers, and [[geisha]], among others.<ref name=":0" />
=== Jurōjin === {{Main|Jurōjin}} Considered the incarnation of the southern [[pole star]] (南極星 "nankyokusei"), Juroujin (寿老人) is the god of the elderly and of longevity in Japanese Buddhist mythology. It is said that the legendary Juroujin is based on a real person who lived in ancient times. He was approximately 1.82 meters tall with a very long head. Besides his distinctive skull, he is represented with a long white beard, riding a deer, and is often also accompanied by a 1500-year-old [[:en:Crane (bird)|crane]] and a tortoise, as symbols of his affinity with long lives. In addition, he is usually represented under a peach tree, as the fruit of this tree is considered, by Chinese Taoism, as able to prolong life. In his hand he holds a cane and a book or a scroll. The wisdom of the world remains written in its pages. Jurojin enjoys rice and wine and is a very cheerful figure.<ref name=":0" />
=== Hotei === {{Main|Budai}} Hotei (布袋) is the god of fortune, guardian of children, patron of [[:en:Diviners|Diviners]] and barmen, and also the god of popularity. He is depicted as a fat, smiling, bald man with a curly moustache. He always appears half-naked, as his clothes are not wide enough to cover his enormous belly. He blessed the Chinese, and they nicknamed him "Cho-Tei-Shi" or "Ho-Tei-Shi", which means ‘bag of old clothes’.
Hotei was a Zen [[priest]], but his appearance and some of his actions were against their moral code: his appearance made him look like quite a mischievous person and he had no fixed place to sleep.
He carries a bag on his shoulders which is loaded with fortunes for those who believe in his virtues. Hotei's traits and virtues are contentment, magnanimity, and happiness.
Hotei's original Chinese name was Kaishi, and according to legend, he died in March 916.
The Japanese began to believe in Hotei during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo era]]. The reason why the Japanese have such great respect for this god comes from a legend that says that, before Zen Buddhism arrived in Japan, an alternative Buddhist thought was extended by a priest of dubious aesthetic, who actually was a manifestation of [[Maitreya|Miroku]]. Miroku was the patron of those who could not be saved by the beliefs of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]], and Hotei was later perceived and accepted by the Japanese as a second Miroku.<ref name=":0" />
=== Fukurokuju (sometimes omitted) === {{Main|Fukurokuju}} The god Fukurokuju (福禄寿) also has his origins in [[:en:China|China]]. It is believed that he used to be a [[hermit]] during the Chinese [[Song dynasty]], distinguished for being a reincarnation of the Taoist god Hsuan-wu. He is the god of wisdom, luck, longevity, wealth and happiness. This god receives certain credits, such as being one of the Chinese philosophers who could live without eating (breatharian). Moreover, he is the only god who was said to have the ability to resurrect the dead. Fukurokuju is characterized by the size of his head, being almost as large as the size of his whole body, and is represented wearing traditional Chinese costumes. He normally carries a cane in one hand and in the other a [[scroll]] with writings about the world. He is usually accompanied by a turtle, a crow or a deer, animals that are frequently used in Japan to symbolize a long life. It is also said that he likes to play [[:en:Chess|Chess]], and hence is the patron of chess players.<ref name=":0" /> The characteristics of Fukurokuju and Jurōjin overlap as they both trace back to the Chinese Taoist deity [[Old Man of the South Pole|Nánjílǎorén (南极老人)]], which is why Fukurokuju's position is sometimes granted instead to the goddess [[#Kichijōten (吉祥天)|Kichijōten]], as in the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]] of 1783.<ref name="Butsuzōzui077" />
=== Kichijōten (sometimes omitted)===
{{Main|Kisshōten}} Kichijōten (吉祥天), a Fukujin goddess is also known as Kisshōten or Kisshoutennyo (吉祥天女), and is adapted via [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] from the [[Hinduism in Japan|Hindu]] goddess [[Lakshmi]]. Kisshōten has the traits of beauty, happiness, and fertility. In the 1783 edition of the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]], Kichijōten replaces [[#Fukurokuju (福禄寿)|Fukurokuju]] as one of the Fukujin.<ref name="Butsuzōzui077">{{cite web|title=Butsuzōzui (Illustrated Compendium of Buddhist Images)|url=http://www.lib.ehime-u.ac.jp/SUZUKA/316/index.html|location=Ehime University Library|page=(077.jpg)|language=ja|format=digital photos|date=1796}}</ref> Kichijōten's iconography is distinguished from the other Fukujin goddesses by the [[Cintamani|Nyoihōju gem (如意宝珠)]] in her hand. When Kichijōten replaces Fukurokuju, and Daikoku is regarded in feminine form,<ref name="Butsuzōzui059" /> all three of the Hindu [[Tridevi]] goddesses are then represented among the seven Fukujin.
== Takarabune == [[File:Seven Lucky Gods (Shichi fukujin) in Treasure Ship by Utagawa Toyokuni I c1806.png|thumb|230px|Ukiyo-e of the lucky gods on a ''Takarabune'' by [[Utagawa Toyokuni]]]] {{Main|Takarabune}}
During the first three days of the [[New Year]] the Seven Lucky Gods are said to pilot through the heavens the ''[[Takarabune]]'' or Treasure Ship.<ref name="Chiba">{{Cite book |title=The Seven Lucky Gods of Japan |last=Reiko |first=Chiba |date=1966 |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co |pages=9–10 |oclc=40117755}}</ref> A picture of the ship forms an essential part of traditional [[Japanese New Year]] celebrations.<ref name="V&A">{{Cite web |url=https://collections.vam.ac.uk/item/O74843/the-treasure-ship-woodblock-print-utagawa-hiroshige |title=The Treasure Ship |publisher= [[Victoria and Albert Museum]] |access-date=2018-07-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180104014057/https://collections.vam.ac.uk/item/O74843/the-treasure-ship-woodblock-print-utagawa-hiroshige|url-status=dead|archive-date=4 January 2018}}</ref>
==Location of shrines== *Toka Ebisu Shrine, Fukuoka *Nanyo – [[Kanjizai-ji]], Shikoku * [[Nishinomiya Shrine]], Hyōgo * Imamiya Ebisu Shrine, Osaka
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:七福神-Shichifukujin.JPG|The Seven Lucky Gods at [[Watatsumi]] Shrine in [[Tarumi-ku, Kobe]] Image:Kuniyoshi Utagawa, The seven goods of good fortune.jpg|The Seven Lucky Gods in a woodblock print by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] Image:Yoshitoshi The Seven Lucky Gods.jpg|The Seven Lucky Gods, in an 1882 woodblock print by Tsukioka [[Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]] File:Seven Gods of Good Luck.jpg|Brooklyn Museum - Shibata Zeshin (Japanese, 1807−1891). Seven Gods of Good Luck, c. 1885 File:Takarabune by Hiroshige.png|Coloured woodblock print of the ''Takarabune'' by [[Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] </gallery>
== See also == {{Portal|Japan|Mythology}} * [[:en:Fu, Lu, and Shou|Fu, Lu, and Shou]] (福祿壽), three deities of stars from [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese folk religion]], representing auspiciousness, good life and longevity. * [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]], a similar group of [[Xian (Taoism)|Taoist immortals]] * [[He-He Er Xian]] (和合二仙), Immortals of Harmony and Union, associated with happy marriages
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==External links== *{{commonscat-inline|Seven Lucky Gods}} *[http://www.artelino.com/articles/japanese_gods_and_goddesses.asp Information on Japanese deities]

]] ]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese deities of good fortune}} [[File:Shichifukujin by Minzan-Ethno BHM 1995.266.49-P6141072-gradient.jpg|thumb|230px|Netsuke depicting the Seven gods of Fortune, on display at [[:en:Bern Historical Museum|Bern Historical Museum]]]] {{Jmyth infobox}} In Japanese mythology, the '''Seven Lucky gods''' or '''Seven gods of Fortune''' ({{lang|ja|七福神}}, {{lang|ja-latn|shichifukujin}} in [[Japanese language|Japanese]]) are believed to grant good [[luck]] and are often represented in [[netsuke]] and in artworks. One of the seven (Jurōjin) is said to be based on a historical figure.
They all began as remote and impersonal gods, but gradually became much closer canonical figures for certain professions and Japanese [[The arts|arts]]. During the course of their history, the mutual influence between gods has created confusion about which of them was the patron of certain professions. The worship of this group of gods is also due to the importance of the number seven in Japan, supposedly a signifier of good luck.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The seven lucky gods of Japan|last=Chiba|first=Reiko|year=1995}}</ref>
==Origin and history== [[File:Shichi fukujin.jpg|thumb|400px|right|From left to right: Hotei, Jurōjin, Fukurokuju, Bishamonten, Benzaiten, Daikokuten, Ebisu]] It is known that these deities mostly have their origins as ancient gods of fortune from religions popular in Japan: from [[Mahayana Buddhism]] (Benzaiten, Bishamonten, Daikokuten) which came to Japan from China but originated in India, and from Chinese [[Taoism]] (Fukurokuju, Hotei, Jurojin); except for one (Ebisu) who has a native Japanese ancestry.
These gods have been recognized as such for over a thousand years. In the beginning, these gods were worshiped by merchants as the first two (Ebisu and Daikokuten) were gods of [[business]] and trade.
Subsequently, the other classes of Japanese society looked for other gods that could correspond with their professions: Benzaiten as the patron of the arts, Fukurokuju as the patron of the sciences, and so on.
In ancient times, these gods were worshipped separately, but this rarely happens today – only when it is required for the god to act on behalf of the applicant.
The Seven Gods of Fortune started being mentioned as a collective in the year 1420 in Fushimi, in order to imitate the processions of the ''daimyōs'', the [[:en:Feudalism|feudal lords]] of pre-modern Japan.
It is said that the Buddhist priest [[Tenkai]] selected these gods after speaking with the ''[[shōgun]]'' he served, [[Iemitsu Tokugawa]], at the order of seeking whoever possessed the perfect virtues: longevity, fortune, popularity, sincerity, kindness, dignity, and magnanimity.
Shortly after a famous artist of the time, Kano Yasunobu, was ordained to portray these gods for the first time ever.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|year=2009}}</ref>
==Description of the Fukujin deities== [[File:Seven-Lucky-Gods-of-Japan-Hokusai-七福神.png|thumb|230px|Seven Lucky Gods by [[Hokusai]] et al.]] === Ebisu === {{Main|Ebisu (mythology)}} From the period of the gods [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]], Ebisu (恵比寿) is the only one whose origins are purely Japanese. He is the god of prosperity and wealth in business, and of plenitude and abundance in crops, cereals and food in general. He is the patron of [[:en:Fisherman|fishermen]] and therefore is represented with fishermen's costumes such as a typical hat, a fishing rod in his right hand and a fish that can be either a [[:en:Carp|Carp]], a [[hake]], a [[:en:Cod|Cod]]fish or a sea bass, or any large fish, in general, that symbolize abundance in meals (such as a feast or banquet). It is now common to see his figure in restaurants where fish is served in great quantities or in household kitchens.<ref name=":0" />
=== Daikokuten === {{Main|Daikokuten}} Daikokuten (大黒天) is the god of commerce and prosperity, and he is sometimes considered the patron of cooks, farmers and bankers, and a protector of [[:en:Crop|Crop]]s. He is also considered a [[:en:Demon|Demon]] hunter − legend says that the god Daikokuten hung a sacred talisman on the branch of a tree in his garden and, by using this as a trap, he was able to catch a demon. This god is characterized by his smile, by his short legs and by the hat on his head. He is usually depicted with a bag full of valuable objects.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Daikokuten's popular imagery originated as a [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|syncretic conflation]] of the [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[List of death deities#Hindu mythology|death deity]]{{Broken anchor|date=2024-06-01|bot=User:Cewbot/log/20201008/configuration|target_link=List of death deities#Hindu mythology|reason= The anchor (Hindu mythology) [[Special:Diff/871442714|has been deleted]].}} [[Mahākāla]] with the [[Kami|Shinto deity]] [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{cite book|first=Jeremy|last=Roberts|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|publisher=Infobase Publishing|year=2009|page=28|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC|isbn=9781438128023}}</ref> The Japanese name ''Daikoku'' is a direct translation of the Sanskrit name ''Mahākāla'' which means "Great Blackness". Per the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]] of 1690 (reprinted and expanded in 1796), Daikoku can also manifest as a female known as ''Daikokunyo'' (大黒女, lit. "She of Great Blackness") or ''Daikokutennyo'' (大黒天女, lit. "She of Great Blackness of the Heavens").<ref name="Butsuzōzui059">{{cite web|title=Butsuzōzui (Illustrated Compendium of Buddhist Images)|url=http://www.lib.ehime-u.ac.jp/SUZUKA/316/index.html|location=Ehime University Library|page=(059.jpg)|language=ja|format=digital photos|date=1796}}</ref>
=== Bishamonten === {{Main|Vaiśravana}} Bishamonten's (毘沙門天) origins can be traced back to [[Hinduism]], but he has been adopted into Japanese culture. He comes from the Hindu god [[Kubera]] and is also known by the name "[[Vaiśravaṇa|Vaisravana]]".
He is the god of fortune in war and battles, also associated with authority and dignity. He is the protector of those who follow the rules and behave appropriately. As the patron of fighters, he is represented dressed in [[:en:Armour|Armour]] and a helmet, and is often seen carrying a [[pagoda]]. He also acts as the protector of holy sites and important places and wields a spear to fight against the evil spirits. He is usually depicted in illustrations with a hoop of fire.
=== Benzaiten === {{Main|Benzaiten}} Benzaiten's (弁才天 or 弁財天) origin is found in [[Hinduism in Japan|Hinduism]], as she comes from the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]. She is the only female Fukujin in the modern grouping, and may be named in various ways: Benzaiten (弁才天), Benten (弁天), Bentensama (弁天様), or Benzaitennyo (弁才天女). When she was [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|adapted]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], she was given the attributes of financial fortune, talent, beauty and music among others. Often, her figure appears with a ''[[Torii]]''. She is represented as a smart, beautiful woman with all the aforementioned attributes. She carries a ''[[:en:Biwa|Biwa]]'', a Japanese traditional lute-like instrument, and is normally accompanied by a white snake. She is the patron of artists, writers, dancers, and [[:en:Geisha|Geisha]], among others.<ref name=":0" />
=== Jurōjin === {{Main|Jurōjin}} Considered the incarnation of the southern [[pole star]] (南極星 "nankyokusei"), Juroujin (寿老人) is the god of the elderly and of longevity in Japanese Buddhist mythology. It is said that the legendary Juroujin is based on a real person who lived in ancient times. He was approximately 1.82 meters tall with a very long head. Besides his distinctive skull, he is represented with a long white beard, riding a deer, and is often also accompanied by a 1500-year-old [[:en:Crane (bird)|crane]] and a tortoise, as symbols of his affinity with long lives. In addition, he is usually represented under a peach tree, as the fruit of this tree is considered, by Chinese Taoism, as able to prolong life. In his hand he holds a cane and a book or a scroll. The wisdom of the world remains written in its pages. Jurojin enjoys rice and wine and is a very cheerful figure.<ref name=":0" />
=== Hotei === {{Main|Budai}} Hotei (布袋) is the god of fortune, guardian of children, patron of [[:en:Diviners|Diviners]] and barmen, and also the god of popularity. He is depicted as a fat, smiling, bald man with a curly moustache. He always appears half-naked, as his clothes are not wide enough to cover his enormous belly. He blessed the Chinese, and they nicknamed him "Cho-Tei-Shi" or "Ho-Tei-Shi", which means ‘bag of old clothes’.
Hotei was a Zen [[priest]], but his appearance and some of his actions were against their moral code: his appearance made him look like quite a mischievous person and he had no fixed place to sleep.
He carries a bag on his shoulders which is loaded with fortunes for those who believe in his virtues. Hotei's traits and virtues are contentment, magnanimity, and happiness.
Hotei's original Chinese name was Kaishi, and according to legend, he died in March 916.
The Japanese began to believe in Hotei during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo era]]. The reason why the Japanese have such great respect for this god comes from a legend that says that, before Zen Buddhism arrived in Japan, an alternative Buddhist thought was extended by a priest of dubious aesthetic, who actually was a manifestation of [[Maitreya|Miroku]]. Miroku was the patron of those who could not be saved by the beliefs of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]], and Hotei was later perceived and accepted by the Japanese as a second Miroku.<ref name=":0" />
=== Fukurokuju (sometimes omitted) === {{Main|Fukurokuju}} The god Fukurokuju (福禄寿) also has his origins in [[:en:China|China]]. It is believed that he used to be a [[hermit]] during the Chinese [[Song dynasty]], distinguished for being a reincarnation of the Taoist god Hsuan-wu. He is the god of wisdom, luck, longevity, wealth and happiness. This god receives certain credits, such as being one of the Chinese philosophers who could live without eating (breatharian). Moreover, he is the only god who was said to have the ability to resurrect the dead. Fukurokuju is characterized by the size of his head, being almost as large as the size of his whole body, and is represented wearing traditional Chinese costumes. He normally carries a cane in one hand and in the other a [[scroll]] with writings about the world. He is usually accompanied by a turtle, a crow or a deer, animals that are frequently used in Japan to symbolize a long life. It is also said that he likes to play [[:en:Chess|Chess]], and hence is the patron of chess players.<ref name=":0" /> The characteristics of Fukurokuju and Jurōjin overlap as they both trace back to the Chinese Taoist deity [[Old Man of the South Pole|Nánjílǎorén (南极老人)]], which is why Fukurokuju's position is sometimes granted instead to the goddess [[#Kichijōten (吉祥天)|Kichijōten]], as in the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]] of 1783.<ref name="Butsuzōzui077" />
=== Kichijōten (sometimes omitted)===
{{Main|Kisshōten}} Kichijōten (吉祥天), a Fukujin goddess is also known as Kisshōten or Kisshoutennyo (吉祥天女), and is adapted via [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] from the [[Hinduism in Japan|Hindu]] goddess [[Lakshmi]]. Kisshōten has the traits of beauty, happiness, and fertility. In the 1783 edition of the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]], Kichijōten replaces [[#Fukurokuju (福禄寿)|Fukurokuju]] as one of the Fukujin.<ref name="Butsuzōzui077">{{cite web|title=Butsuzōzui (Illustrated Compendium of Buddhist Images)|url=http://www.lib.ehime-u.ac.jp/SUZUKA/316/index.html|location=Ehime University Library|page=(077.jpg)|language=ja|format=digital photos|date=1796}}</ref> Kichijōten's iconography is distinguished from the other Fukujin goddesses by the [[Cintamani|Nyoihōju gem (如意宝珠)]] in her hand. When Kichijōten replaces Fukurokuju, and Daikoku is regarded in feminine form,<ref name="Butsuzōzui059" /> all three of the Hindu [[Tridevi]] goddesses are then represented among the seven Fukujin.
== Takarabune == [[File:Seven Lucky Gods (Shichi fukujin) in Treasure Ship by Utagawa Toyokuni I c1806.png|thumb|230px|Ukiyo-e of the lucky gods on a ''Takarabune'' by [[Utagawa Toyokuni]]]] {{Main|Takarabune}}
During the first three days of the [[New Year]] the Seven Lucky Gods are said to pilot through the heavens the ''[[Takarabune]]'' or Treasure Ship.<ref name="Chiba">{{Cite book |title=The Seven Lucky Gods of Japan |last=Reiko |first=Chiba |date=1966 |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co |pages=9–10 |oclc=40117755}}</ref> A picture of the ship forms an essential part of traditional [[Japanese New Year]] celebrations.<ref name="V&A">{{Cite web |url=https://collections.vam.ac.uk/item/O74843/the-treasure-ship-woodblock-print-utagawa-hiroshige |title=The Treasure Ship |publisher= [[Victoria and Albert Museum]] |access-date=2018-07-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180104014057/https://collections.vam.ac.uk/item/O74843/the-treasure-ship-woodblock-print-utagawa-hiroshige|url-status=dead|archive-date=4 January 2018}}</ref>
==Location of shrines== *Toka Ebisu Shrine, Fukuoka *Nanyo – [[Kanjizai-ji]], Shikoku * [[Nishinomiya Shrine]], Hyōgo * Imamiya Ebisu Shrine, Osaka
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:七福神-Shichifukujin.JPG|The Seven Lucky Gods at [[Watatsumi]] Shrine in [[Tarumi-ku, Kobe]] Image:Kuniyoshi Utagawa, The seven goods of good fortune.jpg|The Seven Lucky Gods in a woodblock print by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] Image:Yoshitoshi The Seven Lucky Gods.jpg|The Seven Lucky Gods, in an 1882 woodblock print by Tsukioka [[Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]] File:Seven Gods of Good Luck.jpg|Brooklyn Museum - Shibata Zeshin (Japanese, 1807−1891). Seven Gods of Good Luck, c. 1885 File:Takarabune by Hiroshige.png|Coloured woodblock print of the ''Takarabune'' by [[Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] </gallery>
== See also == {{Portal|Japan|Mythology}} * [[:en:Fu, Lu, and Shou|Fu, Lu, and Shou]] (福祿壽), three deities of stars from [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese folk religion]], representing auspiciousness, good life and longevity. * [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]], a similar group of [[Xian (Taoism)|Taoist immortals]] * [[He-He Er Xian]] (和合二仙), Immortals of Harmony and Union, associated with happy marriages
==Notes== {{reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese deities of good fortune}} [[File:Shichifukujin by Minzan-Ethno BHM 1995.266.49-P6141072-gradient.jpg|thumb|230px|Netsuke depicting the Seven gods of Fortune, on display at [[:en:Bern Historical Museum|Bern Historical Museum]]]] {{Jmyth infobox}} In Japanese mythology, the '''Seven Lucky gods''' or '''Seven gods of Fortune''' ({{lang|ja|七福神}}, {{lang|ja-latn|shichifukujin}} in [[Japanese language|Japanese]]) are believed to grant good [[luck]] and are often represented in [[netsuke]] and in artworks. One of the seven (Jurōjin) is said to be based on a historical figure.
They all began as remote and impersonal gods, but gradually became much closer canonical figures for certain professions and Japanese [[The arts|arts]]. During the course of their history, the mutual influence between gods has created confusion about which of them was the patron of certain professions. The worship of this group of gods is also due to the importance of the number seven in Japan, supposedly a signifier of good luck.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The seven lucky gods of Japan|last=Chiba|first=Reiko|year=1995}}</ref>
==Origin and history== [[File:Shichi fukujin.jpg|thumb|400px|right|From left to right: Hotei, Jurōjin, Fukurokuju, Bishamonten, Benzaiten, Daikokuten, Ebisu]] It is known that these deities mostly have their origins as ancient gods of fortune from religions popular in Japan: from [[Mahayana Buddhism]] (Benzaiten, Bishamonten, Daikokuten) which came to Japan from China but originated in India, and from Chinese [[Taoism]] (Fukurokuju, Hotei, Jurojin); except for one (Ebisu) who has a native Japanese ancestry.
These gods have been recognized as such for over a thousand years. In the beginning, these gods were worshiped by merchants as the first two (Ebisu and Daikokuten) were gods of [[:en:Business|Business]] and trade.
Subsequently, the other classes of Japanese society looked for other gods that could correspond with their professions: Benzaiten as the patron of the arts, Fukurokuju as the patron of the sciences, and so on.
In ancient times, these gods were worshipped separately, but this rarely happens today – only when it is required for the god to act on behalf of the applicant.
The Seven Gods of Fortune started being mentioned as a collective in the year 1420 in Fushimi, in order to imitate the processions of the ''daimyōs'', the [[:en:Feudalism|feudal lords]] of pre-modern Japan.
It is said that the Buddhist priest [[Tenkai]] selected these gods after speaking with the ''[[shōgun]]'' he served, [[Iemitsu Tokugawa]], at the order of seeking whoever possessed the perfect virtues: longevity, fortune, popularity, sincerity, kindness, dignity, and magnanimity.
Shortly after a famous artist of the time, Kano Yasunobu, was ordained to portray these gods for the first time ever.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|year=2009}}</ref>
==Description of the Fukujin deities== [[File:Seven-Lucky-Gods-of-Japan-Hokusai-七福神.png|thumb|230px|Seven Lucky Gods by [[Hokusai]] et al.]] === Ebisu === {{Main|Ebisu (mythology)}} From the period of the gods [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami]] and [[Izanagi]], Ebisu (恵比寿) is the only one whose origins are purely Japanese. He is the god of prosperity and wealth in business, and of plenitude and abundance in crops, cereals and food in general. He is the patron of [[:en:Fisherman|fishermen]] and therefore is represented with fishermen's costumes such as a typical hat, a fishing rod in his right hand and a fish that can be either a [[:en:Carp|Carp]], a [[hake]], a [[:en:Cod|Cod]]fish or a sea bass, or any large fish, in general, that symbolize abundance in meals (such as a feast or banquet). It is now common to see his figure in restaurants where fish is served in great quantities or in household kitchens.<ref name=":0" />
=== Daikokuten === {{Main|Daikokuten}} Daikokuten (大黒天) is the god of commerce and prosperity, and he is sometimes considered the patron of cooks, farmers and bankers, and a protector of [[:en:Crop|Crop]]s. He is also considered a [[:en:Demon|Demon]] hunter − legend says that the god Daikokuten hung a sacred talisman on the branch of a tree in his garden and, by using this as a trap, he was able to catch a demon. This god is characterized by his smile, by his short legs and by the hat on his head. He is usually depicted with a bag full of valuable objects.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Daikokuten's popular imagery originated as a [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|syncretic conflation]] of the [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[List of death deities#Hindu mythology|death deity]]{{Broken anchor|date=2024-06-01|bot=User:Cewbot/log/20201008/configuration|target_link=List of death deities#Hindu mythology|reason= The anchor (Hindu mythology) [[Special:Diff/871442714|has been deleted]].}} [[Mahākāla]] with the [[Kami|Shinto deity]] [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref>{{cite book|first=Jeremy|last=Roberts|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|publisher=Infobase Publishing|year=2009|page=28|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC|isbn=9781438128023}}</ref> The Japanese name ''Daikoku'' is a direct translation of the Sanskrit name ''Mahākāla'' which means "Great Blackness". Per the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]] of 1690 (reprinted and expanded in 1796), Daikoku can also manifest as a female known as ''Daikokunyo'' (大黒女, lit. "She of Great Blackness") or ''Daikokutennyo'' (大黒天女, lit. "She of Great Blackness of the Heavens").<ref name="Butsuzōzui059">{{cite web|title=Butsuzōzui (Illustrated Compendium of Buddhist Images)|url=http://www.lib.ehime-u.ac.jp/SUZUKA/316/index.html|location=Ehime University Library|page=(059.jpg)|language=ja|format=digital photos|date=1796}}</ref>
=== Bishamonten === {{Main|Vaiśravana}} Bishamonten's (毘沙門天) origins can be traced back to [[Hinduism]], but he has been adopted into Japanese culture. He comes from the Hindu god [[Kubera]] and is also known by the name "[[Vaiśravaṇa|Vaisravana]]".
He is the god of fortune in war and battles, also associated with authority and dignity. He is the protector of those who follow the rules and behave appropriately. As the patron of fighters, he is represented dressed in [[:en:Armour|Armour]] and a helmet, and is often seen carrying a [[pagoda]]. He also acts as the protector of holy sites and important places and wields a spear to fight against the evil spirits. He is usually depicted in illustrations with a hoop of fire.
=== Benzaiten === {{Main|Benzaiten}} Benzaiten's (弁才天 or 弁財天) origin is found in [[Hinduism in Japan|Hinduism]], as she comes from the Hindu goddess [[Saraswati]]. She is the only female Fukujin in the modern grouping, and may be named in various ways: Benzaiten (弁才天), Benten (弁天), Bentensama (弁天様), or Benzaitennyo (弁才天女). When she was [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|adapted]] from [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], she was given the attributes of financial fortune, talent, beauty and music among others. Often, her figure appears with a ''[[Torii]]''. She is represented as a smart, beautiful woman with all the aforementioned attributes. She carries a ''[[:en:Biwa|Biwa]]'', a Japanese traditional lute-like instrument, and is normally accompanied by a white snake. She is the patron of artists, writers, dancers, and [[:en:Geisha|Geisha]], among others.<ref name=":0" />
=== Jurōjin === {{Main|Jurōjin}} Considered the incarnation of the southern [[pole star]] (南極星 "nankyokusei"), Juroujin (寿老人) is the god of the elderly and of longevity in Japanese Buddhist mythology. It is said that the legendary Juroujin is based on a real person who lived in ancient times. He was approximately 1.82 meters tall with a very long head. Besides his distinctive skull, he is represented with a long white beard, riding a deer, and is often also accompanied by a 1500-year-old [[:en:Crane (bird)|crane]] and a tortoise, as symbols of his affinity with long lives. In addition, he is usually represented under a peach tree, as the fruit of this tree is considered, by Chinese Taoism, as able to prolong life. In his hand he holds a cane and a book or a scroll. The wisdom of the world remains written in its pages. Jurojin enjoys rice and wine and is a very cheerful figure.<ref name=":0" />
=== Hotei === {{Main|Budai}} Hotei (布袋) is the god of fortune, guardian of children, patron of [[:en:Diviners|Diviners]] and barmen, and also the god of popularity. He is depicted as a fat, smiling, bald man with a curly moustache. He always appears half-naked, as his clothes are not wide enough to cover his enormous belly. He blessed the Chinese, and they nicknamed him "Cho-Tei-Shi" or "Ho-Tei-Shi", which means ‘bag of old clothes’.
Hotei was a Zen [[priest]], but his appearance and some of his actions were against their moral code: his appearance made him look like quite a mischievous person and he had no fixed place to sleep.
He carries a bag on his shoulders which is loaded with fortunes for those who believe in his virtues. Hotei's traits and virtues are contentment, magnanimity, and happiness.
Hotei's original Chinese name was Kaishi, and according to legend, he died in March 916.
The Japanese began to believe in Hotei during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo era]]. The reason why the Japanese have such great respect for this god comes from a legend that says that, before Zen Buddhism arrived in Japan, an alternative Buddhist thought was extended by a priest of dubious aesthetic, who actually was a manifestation of [[Maitreya|Miroku]]. Miroku was the patron of those who could not be saved by the beliefs of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]], and Hotei was later perceived and accepted by the Japanese as a second Miroku.<ref name=":0" />
=== Fukurokuju (sometimes omitted) === {{Main|Fukurokuju}} The god Fukurokuju (福禄寿) also has his origins in [[:en:China|China]]. It is believed that he used to be a [[hermit]] during the Chinese [[Song dynasty]], distinguished for being a reincarnation of the Taoist god Hsuan-wu. He is the god of wisdom, luck, longevity, wealth and happiness. This god receives certain credits, such as being one of the Chinese philosophers who could live without eating (breatharian). Moreover, he is the only god who was said to have the ability to resurrect the dead. Fukurokuju is characterized by the size of his head, being almost as large as the size of his whole body, and is represented wearing traditional Chinese costumes. He normally carries a cane in one hand and in the other a [[scroll]] with writings about the world. He is usually accompanied by a turtle, a crow or a deer, animals that are frequently used in Japan to symbolize a long life. It is also said that he likes to play [[:en:Chess|Chess]], and hence is the patron of chess players.<ref name=":0" /> The characteristics of Fukurokuju and Jurōjin overlap as they both trace back to the Chinese Taoist deity [[Old Man of the South Pole|Nánjílǎorén (南极老人)]], which is why Fukurokuju's position is sometimes granted instead to the goddess [[#Kichijōten (吉祥天)|Kichijōten]], as in the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]] of 1783.<ref name="Butsuzōzui077" />
=== Kichijōten (sometimes omitted)===
{{Main|Kisshōten}} Kichijōten (吉祥天), a Fukujin goddess is also known as Kisshōten or Kisshoutennyo (吉祥天女), and is adapted via [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] from the [[Hinduism in Japan|Hindu]] goddess [[Lakshmi]]. Kisshōten has the traits of beauty, happiness, and fertility. In the 1783 edition of the [[Butsuzōzui|Butsuzōzui compendium]], Kichijōten replaces [[#Fukurokuju (福禄寿)|Fukurokuju]] as one of the Fukujin.<ref name="Butsuzōzui077">{{cite web|title=Butsuzōzui (Illustrated Compendium of Buddhist Images)|url=http://www.lib.ehime-u.ac.jp/SUZUKA/316/index.html|location=Ehime University Library|page=(077.jpg)|language=ja|format=digital photos|date=1796}}</ref> Kichijōten's iconography is distinguished from the other Fukujin goddesses by the [[Cintamani|Nyoihōju gem (如意宝珠)]] in her hand. When Kichijōten replaces Fukurokuju, and Daikoku is regarded in feminine form,<ref name="Butsuzōzui059" /> all three of the Hindu [[Tridevi]] goddesses are then represented among the seven Fukujin.
== Takarabune == [[File:Seven Lucky Gods (Shichi fukujin) in Treasure Ship by Utagawa Toyokuni I c1806.png|thumb|230px|Ukiyo-e of the lucky gods on a ''Takarabune'' by [[Utagawa Toyokuni]]]] {{Main|Takarabune}}
During the first three days of the [[New Year]] the Seven Lucky Gods are said to pilot through the heavens the ''[[Takarabune]]'' or Treasure Ship.<ref name="Chiba">{{Cite book |title=The Seven Lucky Gods of Japan |last=Reiko |first=Chiba |date=1966 |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co |pages=9–10 |oclc=40117755}}</ref> A picture of the ship forms an essential part of traditional [[Japanese New Year]] celebrations.<ref name="V&A">{{Cite web |url=https://collections.vam.ac.uk/item/O74843/the-treasure-ship-woodblock-print-utagawa-hiroshige |title=The Treasure Ship |publisher= [[Victoria and Albert Museum]] |access-date=2018-07-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180104014057/https://collections.vam.ac.uk/item/O74843/the-treasure-ship-woodblock-print-utagawa-hiroshige|url-status=dead|archive-date=4 January 2018}}</ref>
==Location of shrines== *Toka Ebisu Shrine, Fukuoka *Nanyo – [[Kanjizai-ji]], Shikoku * [[Nishinomiya Shrine]], Hyōgo * Imamiya Ebisu Shrine, Osaka
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:七福神-Shichifukujin.JPG|The Seven Lucky Gods at [[Watatsumi]] Shrine in [[Tarumi-ku, Kobe]] Image:Kuniyoshi Utagawa, The seven goods of good fortune.jpg|The Seven Lucky Gods in a woodblock print by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] Image:Yoshitoshi The Seven Lucky Gods.jpg|The Seven Lucky Gods, in an 1882 woodblock print by Tsukioka [[Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]] File:Seven Gods of Good Luck.jpg|Brooklyn Museum - Shibata Zeshin (Japanese, 1807−1891). Seven Gods of Good Luck, c. 1885 File:Takarabune by Hiroshige.png|Coloured woodblock print of the ''Takarabune'' by [[Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] </gallery>
== See also == {{Portal|Japan|Mythology}} * [[:en:Fu, Lu, and Shou|Fu, Lu, and Shou]] (福祿壽), three deities of stars from [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese folk religion]], representing auspiciousness, good life and longevity. * [[:en:Eight Immortals|Eight Immortals]], a similar group of [[Xian (Taoism)|Taoist immortals]] * [[He-He Er Xian]] (和合二仙), Immortals of Harmony and Union, associated with happy marriages
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==External links== *{{commonscat-inline|Seven Lucky Gods}} *[http://www.artelino.com/articles/japanese_gods_and_goddesses.asp Information on Japanese deities]
{{Jmyth navbox long}}
]] ]]

[[sn:Seven Lucky Gods]] [[en:Seven Lucky Gods]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:آلهة الحظ السبعة]] [[az:Yeddi Şanslı Tanrı]] [[ca:Set Déus de la Fortuna]] [[cs:Sedm bohů štěstí]] [[de:Sieben Glücksgötter]] [[es:Siete Dioses de la Fortuna]] [[fa:هفت خدای بخت و اقبال]] [[fi:Shichifukujin]] [[fr:Sept divinités du bonheur]] [[hr:Shichi Fukujin]] [[hu:Hét szerencseisten]] [[hy:Երջանկության յոթ աստվածներ]] [[it:Sette divinità della fortuna]] [[ja:七福神]] [[ko:칠복신]] [[my:ကံကြမ္မာနတ်ဘုရား ခုနစ်ပါး]] [[nl:Shichi Fukujin]] [[pl:Shichi-fukujin]] [[pt:Sete Deuses da Sorte]] [[ro:Cei șapte zei ai norocului]] [[ru:Семь богов счастья]] [[sh:Sedam božanstava sreće]] [[simple:Seven Lucky Gods]] [[th:เจ็ดเทพเจ้าโชคลาภ]] [[tr:Yedi Şanslı Tanrı]] [[uk:Сім богів щастя]] [[vi:Thất Phúc Thần]] [[zh:七福神]] [[zh-min-nan:Chhit-hok-sîn]]
